items.title: Yserver: Modern X11 Server Written in Rust with the Help of Claude Code
items.why_it_matters: A developer successfully built a highly complex, low-level X11 server in Rust by directing Claude Code to do the heavy lifting. This proves that 'vibe coding' is fully capable of shipping production-grade, complex systems if you direct the AI effectively. This week, open Claude Code and task it with refactoring your trickiest Supabase database schema or query, letting the AI handle the complex syntax while you focus on directing the architecture.

items.title: Show HN: ShellShot – Send screenshots and screen recordings into Claude Code
items.why_it_matters: This open-source tool lets you send screenshots and screen recordings directly into Claude Code from your terminal. As a frontend developer using Next.js, explaining visual bugs to a terminal-based AI is tedious; this tool lets you feed visual context straight to Claude Code instantly. This week, install ShellShot via npm and use it to send a screenshot of a broken UI state in your Next.js app directly to Claude Code to get an instant CSS fix.

items.title: Claude Fable is relentlessly proactive
items.why_it_matters: Simon Willison documented how Anthropic's new Claude Fable model actively anticipates user needs and takes initiative during tasks rather than waiting for step-by-step instructions. Understanding this behavior helps you get better at the 'direction' part of vibe coding, knowing when to let the model take the lead. This week, switch your AI-ingest prompt to run on Claude Fable and see if its proactive behavior improves how it filters and categorizes your news feed.

items.title: Running Claude Code Offline on an M3 Pro with Qwen3.6
items.why_it_matters: A developer documented how to run Claude Code offline on an M3 Pro Mac using the open-source Qwen model. This is highly relevant if you want to drastically cut your API costs and avoid Anthropic's rate limits by routing Claude Code's background tasks to a free, locally-run model. This week, follow the guide to set up a local Ollama instance with Qwen and configure Claude Code to use it for non-critical coding tasks to save on your token budget.

items.title: How HN: I gave Claude live Intel on 3k AI companies (teahose-mcp)
items.why_it_matters: A developer released an MCP server that gives Claude live, searchable intelligence on 3,000 AI companies, including their mentions and funding. It shows how easily you can extend Claude Code's capabilities using Model Context Protocol (MCP) to bring external data into your workspace. This week, add this MCP server to your local Claude Code configuration file so you can query market data directly from your terminal while coding.

items.title: Show HN: Eidentic – TypeScript SDK for AI agents with self-improving memory
items.why_it_matters: Eidentic is a new TypeScript SDK that gives AI agents self-improving, persistent memory across sessions. It provides a ready-to-use package for your Next.js and Supabase stack to stop your AI projects from suffering from 'amnesia' without you writing complex memory sync logic. This week, install the `eidentic` package in your AI-ingest repo and use it to let the app remember which news topics you consistently ignore.

items.title: Show HN: Deploy personal apps with your agent via Buildy
items.why_it_matters: Buildy is a platform that lets you deploy LLM-generated personal apps with persistent KV storage and an MCP server in one click. It removes the friction of setting up auth, databases, and hosting when you just want to quickly test a small AI-driven side project. This week, use Claude Code to generate a simple single-file utility script, and deploy it to Buildy to see how quickly you can go from an idea to a live URL.
